---
title: "Candyroot (Senega nana)"
slug: "senega-nana"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/625195892/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Candyroot"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Fabales"
tax_family: "Polygalaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Fabales", "Polygalaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/625195892/medium.jpg" alt="Candyroot" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/sebento">
sebento</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/343241026">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Fabales
-   **Family:** Polygalaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=1568926)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Common species in Florida found in dry, sandy habitats.

## Natural history notes

A small wildflower characterized by its low-growing habit, narrow leaves, and clusters of tiny pink to purplish flowers. In Florida, it is found in dry, sandy habitats such as scrub, sandhills, and open pine flatwoods. It grows during the warmer months and is often a subtle component of groundcover vegetation in fire-maintained ecosystems.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/1568926-Senega-nana)
-   [Local profile](https://www.flawildflowers.org/flower-friday-senega-nana/)
